agent_output: {"executive_summary":"Orchestration-based saga for a 4-step courier marketplace order flow (Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier). A central Saga Orchestrator drives the happy path forward and, on any step failure, executes compensating transactions in reverse order. This gives explicit, auditable control flow with clear failure semantics and avoids the debugging nightmare of choreography-based sagas at this complexity level.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"SagaOrchestrator","responsibility":"Drives the order saga forward step-by-step, persists saga state to a durable store, and triggers compensating transactions on failure. Owns the saga execution log.","boundaries":"Knows the saga definition (step order + compensations) but contains ZERO business logic. Does not know how to reserve inventory or charge a card — only knows which service to call and what to call if it fails."},{"name":"OrderService","responsibility":"Creates and manages order records. Exposes createOrder (returns orderId, status=PENDING) and cancelOrder (sets status=CANCELLED) as the compensating action.","boundaries":"Owns the Order aggregate. Does not know about inventory, payment, or couriers."},{"name":"InventoryService","responsibility":"Manages stock levels and reservations. Exposes reserveItems (soft-locks inventory for an order) and releaseReservation (compensating action that frees the soft-lock).","boundaries":"Owns the Inventory/StockReservation aggregate. Reservation has a TTL as a safety net — if the saga never completes or compensates, the reservation auto-expires."},{"name":"PaymentService","responsibility":"Charges customers. Exposes chargeCustomer (captures payment) and refundPayment (compensating action). Uses an idempotency key derived from orderId to prevent double-charges on retries.","boundaries":"Owns the Payment aggregate. Interacts with external payment gateway. Compensating action is a full refund, not a void, because capture has already occurred by the time downstream steps run."},{"name":"CourierService","responsibility":"Assigns an available courier to the order. Exposes assignCourier and unassignCourier (compensating action that releases the courier back to the available pool).","boundaries":"Owns the CourierAssignment aggregate. Assignment is tentative until the saga marks it confirmed."},{"name":"SagaStateStore","responsibility":"Durable persistence of saga instance state: current step, step outcomes, compensation progress. Enables recovery after orchestrator crashes.","boundaries":"Simple key-value or relational store. The orchestrator is the sole writer. Read by monitoring/ops dashboards."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"OrderService","contract":"createOrder(customerId, items) → {orderId, status} | cancelOrder(orderId) → {status: CANCELLED}","coupling_type":"temporal — synchronous request/reply via HTTP or gRPC"},{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"InventoryService","contract":"reserveItems(orderId, items[]) → {reservationId} | releaseReservation(reservationId) → {released: true}","coupling_type":"temporal — synchronous request/reply"},{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"PaymentService","contract":"chargeCustomer(orderId, customerId, amount, idempotencyKey) → {paymentId} | refundPayment(paymentId) → {refundId}","coupling_type":"temporal — synchronous request/reply"},{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"CourierService","contract":"assignCourier(orderId, pickupLocation, deliveryLocation) → {assignmentId, courierId} | unassignCourier(assignmentId) → {released: true}","coupling_type":"temporal — synchronous request/reply"},{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"SagaStateStore","contract":"save(sagaId, state) / load(sagaId) → SagaState","coupling_type":"data — direct persistence"}],"data_flow":"1. Client submits order request → SagaOrchestrator creates a new saga instance (status: STARTED), persists to SagaStateStore. 2. Orchestrator calls OrderService.createOrder → on success, persists step result (orderId), advances state to INVENTORY_PENDING. 3. Orchestrator calls InventoryService.reserveItems → on success, persists reservationId, advances to PAYMENT_PENDING. 4. Orchestrator calls PaymentService.chargeCustomer → on success, persists paymentId, advances to COURIER_PENDING. 5. Orchestrator calls CourierService.assignCourier → on success, persists assignmentId, advances to COMPLETED. On ANY step failure: orchestrator transitions to COMPENSATING, then calls compensating actions in strict reverse order of completed steps, persisting each compensation result. Final state is either COMPLETED or COMPENSATED (or COMPENSATION_FAILED if a compensating action fails after retries — requires manual intervention).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestration Saga","rationale":"With 4 sequential steps and strict reverse-order compensation, a central orchestrator provides explicit control flow, easy-to-reason-about failure handling, and a single place to observe saga state. Choreography would scatter the compensation logic across 4 services and make failure recovery extremely hard to debug."},{"pattern":"Compensating Transactions","rationale":"Distributed transactions (2PC) are impractical across 4 independent services. Each service instead exposes an explicit undo operation. This is semantically weaker than ACID rollback (compensations are visible to other transactions) but is the practical standard for microservice sagas."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Both forward and compensating actions must be idempotent. Network failures and orchestrator restarts will cause retries. Idempotency keys (derived from sagaId + stepName) prevent duplicate side effects like double-charging a customer."},{"pattern":"Saga State Machine","rationale":"Modeling the saga as an explicit state machine (STARTED → ORDER_PENDING → INVENTORY_PENDING → PAYMENT_PENDING → COURIER_PENDING → COMPLETED, with parallel COMPENSATING states) makes the orchestrator's behavior deterministic and recoverable after crashes."},{"pattern":"Reservation with TTL","rationale":"Inventory reservations carry a time-to-live. If the saga hangs or the orchestrator dies without compensating, the reservation auto-expires. This is a safety net against resource leaks — defense in depth beyond the orchestrator's own compensation logic."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"OrderManagement","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: PENDING → CONFIRMED | CANCELLED, contains: line items, customer reference)"]},{"name":"InventoryManagement","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["StockReservation (root: reservationId, contains: itemId, quantity, orderId, expiresAt, status: HELD | RELEASED | COMMITTED)","StockItem (root: itemId, contains: availableQty, reservedQty)"]},{"name":"PaymentProcessing","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId, contains: orderId, amount, status: CAPTURED | REFUNDED, idempotencyKey)"]},{"name":"CourierAssignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (root: assignmentId, contains: orderId, courierId, status: TENTATIVE | CONFIRMED | RELEASED)"]},{"name":"SagaCoordination","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["SagaInstance (root: sagaId, contains: currentStep, stepResults[], compensationProgress, status, createdAt, updatedAt)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"SagaCoordination","downstream":"OrderManagement","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Orchestrator tells OrderService what to do; OrderService owns its own model but conforms to the orchestrator's commands."},{"upstream":"SagaCoordination","downstream":"InventoryManagement","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"SagaCoordination","downstream":"PaymentProcessing","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"SagaCoordination","downstream":"CourierAssignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestration over Choreography","gains":"Single point of observability for the entire saga. Compensation order is explicit and deterministic. Easy to add/remove/reorder steps. Straightforward debugging — read the saga log.","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination bottleneck and single point of failure (mitigated by stateless orchestrator + durable state store). Slightly higher coupling — orchestrator must know all participants.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga: each service emits events and the next service reacts. Rejected because with 4 steps and reverse-order compensation, the event chains become tangled and hard to reason about. Debugging a failed compensation across 4 event-driven services is painful.","Hybrid: orchestrate critical path, choreograph notifications. Considered viable for future evolution but unnecessary complexity now."]},{"decision":"Synchronous request/reply between orchestrator and services","gains":"Simpler mental model. Orchestrator knows immediately if a step succeeded or failed. No need for correlation IDs across async messages for the core flow.","sacrifices":"Temporal coupling — if a service is down, the saga blocks. Mitigated by retries with exponential backoff and circuit breakers.","alternatives_considered":["Async command/reply via message broker: more resilient to transient failures but adds infrastructure complexity (broker, dead-letter queues, correlation). Worth adopting later if service availability becomes an issue."]},{"decision":"Saga state persisted after every step","gains":"Crash recovery — orchestrator can restart and resume from last persisted state. Auditability — full history of what happened.","sacrifices":"One extra write per step (4-8 writes per saga). Acceptable latency cost for the durability guarantee.","alternatives_considered":["In-memory only state: faster but non-recoverable. Rejected — losing saga state mid-compensation could leave the system in an inconsistent state permanently."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Step ordering","evolution_path":"The saga definition is a data structure (ordered list of steps with forward/compensate actions). Reordering steps or inserting new ones (e.g., a FraudCheck step between Payment and Courier) requires changing only the saga definition, not the services."},{"area":"Sync-to-async migration","evolution_path":"Each orchestrator-to-service call is behind an interface. Swapping synchronous HTTP calls for async message-based command/reply requires changing only the adapter layer, not the saga logic."},{"area":"Parallel steps","evolution_path":"If future requirements allow inventory reservation and payment to happen in parallel (independent steps), the saga engine can be extended to support parallel step execution with a join before the next sequential step."},{"area":"New services","evolution_path":"Adding a 5th step (e.g., NotificationService) means adding one entry to the saga step list and implementing the forward + compensate operations in the new service. Zero changes to existing services."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Compensation ordering","reason":"Compensating transactions MUST execute in strict reverse order of completed forward steps. This is a correctness invariant, not a design choice. Violating it (e.g., cancelling the order before releasing inventory) could leave dangling reservations or orphaned payments."},{"area":"Idempotency requirement","reason":"Every forward and compensating operation MUST be idempotent. This is non-negotiable — the orchestrator will retry on transient failures. Services that cannot guarantee idempotency will corrupt state on retries."},{"area":"Saga state durability","reason":"Saga state MUST be persisted to durable storage before executing each step. In-memory-only state is not acceptable — an orchestrator crash mid-saga would leave the system in an unrecoverable inconsistent state."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensating action itself fails (e.g., PaymentService is down when we try to refund)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Retry compensating actions with exponential backoff up to a configurable limit (e.g., 10 retries over 30 minutes). If all retries exhausted, mark saga as COMPENSATION_FAILED and alert ops for manual intervention. Never silently give up on compensation."},{"risk":"Orchestrator crashes mid-saga","severity":"high","mitigation":"Saga state is persisted after every step transition. On restart, the orchestrator loads all ACTIVE and COMPENSATING sagas and resumes from their last persisted state. Idempotent operations ensure replaying the current step is safe."},{"risk":"Semantic inconsistency window — a customer sees a charge before courier assignment completes or fails","severity":"medium","mitigation":"This is inherent to sagas (no distributed ACID). Mitigate by keeping the window short (fast step execution), and by showing the order as 'Processing' until the saga reaches COMPLETED. If compensation occurs, proactively notify the customer that the charge will be refunded."},{"risk":"Inventory reservation leak — saga hangs and reservation is never released","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Reservation TTL as defense-in-depth. A background job in InventoryService sweeps expired reservations. Additionally, a saga timeout in the orchestrator triggers compensation if the saga has been active too long."},{"risk":"Duplicate courier assignment due to retry","severity":"medium","mitigation":"CourierService.assignCourier must be idempotent on (orderId). If called twice for the same order, return the existing assignment rather than creating a new one."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement the saga orchestrator as a lightweight state machine. Define the saga as a configuration object: an ordered array of steps, each with { name, forwardAction, compensateAction, timeout }. The orchestrator loop: load current state → execute next forward step → persist result → advance. On failure: transition to COMPENSATING → execute compensating actions in reverse → persist each → finalize as COMPENSATED. Key implementation detail: the orchestrator should be stateless (all state in the store), allowing horizontal scaling and crash recovery.","files_to_create_or_modify":["saga/SagaDefinition.ts — defines the step sequence, forward/compensate action references","saga/SagaOrchestrator.ts — state machine engine that drives execution and compensation","saga/SagaStateStore.ts — persistence interface + implementation (Postgres recommended)","saga/SagaInstance.ts — domain object representing a running saga instance","saga/steps/CreateOrderStep.ts — forward: createOrder, compensate: cancelOrder","saga/steps/ReserveInventoryStep.ts — forward: reserveItems, compensate: releaseReservation","saga/steps/ChargePaymentStep.ts — forward: chargeCustomer, compensate: refundPayment","saga/steps/AssignCourierStep.ts — forward: assignCourier, compensate: unassignCourier","saga/SagaRecoveryJob.ts — background job that resumes stuck sagas on orchestrator restart"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each step implements a SagaStep interface: { execute(context): Promise<StepResult>, compensate(context): Promise<void> }. Context carries accumulated results from prior steps (orderId, reservationId, paymentId). All operations use idempotency keys derived from sagaId + stepName. Compensating actions are fire-and-retry — they must eventually succeed or escalate to manual intervention.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use choreography for the core compensation flow — it becomes untraceable with 4+ services","Do NOT skip persisting saga state between steps — crash recovery depends on it","Do NOT assume compensating actions will succeed on first try — always implement retry with backoff","Do NOT mix saga coordination logic with business logic — the orchestrator should be a generic engine","Do NOT forget idempotency keys on PaymentService calls — double-charging a customer is the highest-severity business failure","Do NOT use database transactions spanning multiple services — that defeats the entire purpose of the saga pattern","Do NOT allow partial compensation — if compensation of step N fails, do NOT proceed to compensate step N-1. Retry step N's compensation first. Out-of-order compensation creates inconsistent state."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestration over choreography for this step count and failure complexity","Compensating transaction design and ordering","Idempotency requirements across all operations","State machine representation of the saga lifecycle"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether sync or async communication fits the actual latency/availability requirements — depends on infrastructure context not provided","Exact TTL values for inventory reservations — domain-specific tuning needed","Whether the payment model should use authorize-then-capture (2-step) vs direct capture — depends on payment gateway capabilities"],"assumptions":["Each service is independently deployable with its own data store","Network between services is unreliable (standard distributed systems assumption)","The orchestrator has access to a durable persistence layer (e.g., Postgres, DynamoDB)","Payment capture is a single-step operation (not authorize + capture) — if 2-step, the saga design simplifies because authorization is easier to void than a capture is to refund","Order volumes are moderate enough that synchronous orchestration does not become a bottleneck — at very high scale, async command/reply would be preferred"]}}
agent_output_excerpt: {"executive_summary":"Orchestration-based saga for a 4-step courier marketplace order flow (Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier). A central Saga Orchestrator drives the happy path forward and, on any step failure, executes compensating transactions in reverse order. This gives explicit, auditable control flow with clear failure semantics and avoids the debugging nightmare of choreography-based sagas at this complexity level.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"SagaOrchestrator","responsibi

content.thinking:
Design a saga pattern for a courier marketplace order flow with 4 services. Let me think through this carefully.

The flow: Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier, with compensating transactions for each step.

This is a classic orchestration saga. Let me design it.

Key implementation detail: the orchestrator should be stateless (all state in the store), allowing horizontal scaling and crash recovery.","files_to_create_or_modify":["saga/SagaDefinition.ts — defines the step sequence, forward/compensate action references","saga/SagaOrchestrator.ts — state machine engine that drives execution and compensation","saga/SagaStateStore.ts — persistence interface + implementation (Postgres recommended)","saga/SagaInstance.ts — domain object representing a running saga instance","saga/steps/CreateOrderStep.ts — forward: createOrder, compensate: cancelOrder","saga/steps/ReserveInventoryStep.ts — forward: reserveItems, compensate: releaseReservation","saga/steps/ChargePaymentStep.ts — forward: chargeCustomer, compensate: refundPayment","saga/steps/AssignCourierStep.ts — forward: assignCourier, compensate: unassignCourier","saga/SagaRecoveryJob.ts — background job that resumes stuck sagas on orchestrator restart"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each step implements a SagaStep interface: { execute(context): Promise<StepResult>, compensate(context): Promise<void> }. Compensating actions are fire-and-retry — they must eventually succeed or escalate to manual intervention.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use choreography for the core compensation flow — it becomes untraceable with 4+ services","Do NOT skip persisting saga state between steps — crash recovery depends on it","Do NOT assume compensating actions will succeed on first try — always implement retry with backoff","Do NOT mix saga coordination logic with business logic — the orchestrator should be a generic engine","Do NOT forget idempotency keys on PaymentService calls — double-charging a customer is the highest-severity business failure","Do NOT use database transactions spanning multiple services — that defeats the entire purpose of the saga pattern","Do NOT allow partial compensation — if compensation of step N fails, do NOT proceed to compensate step N-1. Retry step N's compensation first. Out-of-order compensation creates inconsistent state."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestration over choreography for this step count and failure complexity","Compensating transaction design and ordering","Idempotency requirements across all operations","State machine representation of the saga lifecycle"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether sync or async communication fits the actual latency/availability requirements — depends on infrastructure context not provided","Exact TTL values for inventory reservations — domain-specific tuning needed","Whether the payment model should use authorize-then-capture (2-step) vs direct capture — depends on payment gateway capabilities"],"assumptions":["Each service is independently deployable with its own data store","Network between services is unreliable (standard distributed systems assumption)","The orchestrator has access to a durable persistence layer (e.g., Postgres, DynamoDB)","Payment capture is a single-step operation (not authorize + capture) — if 2-step, the saga design simplifies because authorization is easier to void than a capture is to refund","Order volumes are moderate enough that synchronous orchestration does not become a bottleneck — at very high scale, async command/reply would be preferred"]}}

trace.result: {"executive_summary":"Orchestration-based saga for a 4-step courier marketplace order flow (Order → Inventory → Payment → Courier). A central Saga Orchestrator drives the happy path forward and, on any step failure, executes compensating transactions in reverse order. This gives explicit, auditable control flow with clear failure semantics and avoids the debugging nightmare of choreography-based sagas at this complexity level.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"SagaOrchestrator","responsibility":"Drives the order saga forward step-by-step, persists saga state to a durable store, and triggers compensating transactions on failure. Owns the saga execution log.","boundaries":"Knows the saga definition (step order + compensations) but contains ZERO business logic. Does not know how to reserve inventory or charge a card — only knows which service to call and what to call if it fails."},{"name":"OrderService","responsibility":"Creates and manages order records. Exposes createOrder (returns orderId, status=PENDING) and cancelOrder (sets status=CANCELLED) as the compensating action.","boundaries":"Owns the Order aggregate. Does not know about inventory, payment, or couriers."},{"name":"InventoryService","responsibility":"Manages stock levels and reservations. Exposes reserveItems (soft-locks inventory for an order) and releaseReservation (compensating action that frees the soft-lock).","boundaries":"Owns the Inventory/StockReservation aggregate. Reservation has a TTL as a safety net — if the saga never completes or compensates, the reservation auto-expires."},{"name":"PaymentService","responsibility":"Charges customers. Exposes chargeCustomer (captures payment) and refundPayment (compensating action). Uses an idempotency key derived from orderId to prevent double-charges on retries.","boundaries":"Owns the Payment aggregate. Interacts with external payment gateway. Compensating action is a full refund, not a void, because capture has already occurred by the time downstream steps run."},{"name":"CourierService","responsibility":"Assigns an available courier to the order. Exposes assignCourier and unassignCourier (compensating action that releases the courier back to the available pool).","boundaries":"Owns the CourierAssignment aggregate. Assignment is tentative until the saga marks it confirmed."},{"name":"SagaStateStore","responsibility":"Durable persistence of saga instance state: current step, step outcomes, compensation progress. Enables recovery after orchestrator crashes.","boundaries":"Simple key-value or relational store. The orchestrator is the sole writer. Read by monitoring/ops dashboards."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"OrderService","contract":"createOrder(customerId, items) → {orderId, status} | cancelOrder(orderId) → {status: CANCELLED}","coupling_type":"temporal — synchronous request/reply via HTTP or gRPC"},{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"InventoryService","contract":"reserveItems(orderId, items[]) → {reservationId} | releaseReservation(reservationId) → {released: true}","coupling_type":"temporal — synchronous request/reply"},{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"PaymentService","contract":"chargeCustomer(orderId, customerId, amount, idempotencyKey) → {paymentId} | refundPayment(paymentId) → {refundId}","coupling_type":"temporal — synchronous request/reply"},{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"CourierService","contract":"assignCourier(orderId, pickupLocation, deliveryLocation) → {assignmentId, courierId} | unassignCourier(assignmentId) → {released: true}","coupling_type":"temporal — synchronous request/reply"},{"from":"SagaOrchestrator","to":"SagaStateStore","contract":"save(sagaId, state) / load(sagaId) → SagaState","coupling_type":"data — direct persistence"}],"data_flow":"1. Client submits order request → SagaOrchestrator creates a new saga instance (status: STARTED), persists to SagaStateStore. 2. Orchestrator calls OrderService.createOrder → on success, persists step result (orderId), advances state to INVENTORY_PENDING. 3. Orchestrator calls InventoryService.reserveItems → on success, persists reservationId, advances to PAYMENT_PENDING. 4. Orchestrator calls PaymentService.chargeCustomer → on success, persists paymentId, advances to COURIER_PENDING. 5. Orchestrator calls CourierService.assignCourier → on success, persists assignmentId, advances to COMPLETED. On ANY step failure: orchestrator transitions to COMPENSATING, then calls compensating actions in strict reverse order of completed steps, persisting each compensation result. Final state is either COMPLETED or COMPENSATED (or COMPENSATION_FAILED if a compensating action fails after retries — requires manual intervention).","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Orchestration Saga","rationale":"With 4 sequential steps and strict reverse-order compensation, a central orchestrator provides explicit control flow, easy-to-reason-about failure handling, and a single place to observe saga state. Choreography would scatter the compensation logic across 4 services and make failure recovery extremely hard to debug."},{"pattern":"Compensating Transactions","rationale":"Distributed transactions (2PC) are impractical across 4 independent services. Each service instead exposes an explicit undo operation. This is semantically weaker than ACID rollback (compensations are visible to other transactions) but is the practical standard for microservice sagas."},{"pattern":"Idempotent Operations","rationale":"Both forward and compensating actions must be idempotent. Network failures and orchestrator restarts will cause retries. Idempotency keys (derived from sagaId + stepName) prevent duplicate side effects like double-charging a customer."},{"pattern":"Saga State Machine","rationale":"Modeling the saga as an explicit state machine (STARTED → ORDER_PENDING → INVENTORY_PENDING → PAYMENT_PENDING → COURIER_PENDING → COMPLETED, with parallel COMPENSATING states) makes the orchestrator's behavior deterministic and recoverable after crashes."},{"pattern":"Reservation with TTL","rationale":"Inventory reservations carry a time-to-live. If the saga hangs or the orchestrator dies without compensating, the reservation auto-expires. This is a safety net against resource leaks — defense in depth beyond the orchestrator's own compensation logic."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"OrderManagement","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order (root: orderId, states: PENDING → CONFIRMED | CANCELLED, contains: line items, customer reference)"]},{"name":"InventoryManagement","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["StockReservation (root: reservationId, contains: itemId, quantity, orderId, expiresAt, status: HELD | RELEASED | COMMITTED)","StockItem (root: itemId, contains: availableQty, reservedQty)"]},{"name":"PaymentProcessing","classification":"generic","aggregates":["Payment (root: paymentId, contains: orderId, amount, status: CAPTURED | REFUNDED, idempotencyKey)"]},{"name":"CourierAssignment","classification":"core","aggregates":["Assignment (root: assignmentId, contains: orderId, courierId, status: TENTATIVE | CONFIRMED | RELEASED)"]},{"name":"SagaCoordination","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["SagaInstance (root: sagaId, contains: currentStep, stepResults[], compensationProgress, status, createdAt, updatedAt)"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"SagaCoordination","downstream":"OrderManagement","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Orchestrator tells OrderService what to do; OrderService owns its own model but conforms to the orchestrator's commands."},{"upstream":"SagaCoordination","downstream":"InventoryManagement","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"SagaCoordination","downstream":"PaymentProcessing","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"},{"upstream":"SagaCoordination","downstream":"CourierAssignment","relationship":"Customer-Supplier"}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Orchestration over Choreography","gains":"Single point of observability for the entire saga. Compensation order is explicit and deterministic. Easy to add/remove/reorder steps. Straightforward debugging — read the saga log.","sacrifices":"Orchestrator is a coordination bottleneck and single point of failure (mitigated by stateless orchestrator + durable state store). Slightly higher coupling — orchestrator must know all participants.","alternatives_considered":["Choreography-based saga: each service emits events and the next service reacts. Rejected because with 4 steps and reverse-order compensation, the event chains become tangled and hard to reason about. Debugging a failed compensation across 4 event-driven services is painful.","Hybrid: orchestrate critical path, choreograph notifications. Considered viable for future evolution but unnecessary complexity now."]},{"decision":"Synchronous request/reply between orchestrator and services","gains":"Simpler mental model. Orchestrator knows immediately if a step succeeded or failed. No need for correlation IDs across async messages for the core flow.","sacrifices":"Temporal coupling — if a service is down, the saga blocks. Mitigated by retries with exponential backoff and circuit breakers.","alternatives_considered":["Async command/reply via message broker: more resilient to transient failures but adds infrastructure complexity (broker, dead-letter queues, correlation). Worth adopting later if service availability becomes an issue."]},{"decision":"Saga state persisted after every step","gains":"Crash recovery — orchestrator can restart and resume from last persisted state. Auditability — full history of what happened.","sacrifices":"One extra write per step (4-8 writes per saga). Acceptable latency cost for the durability guarantee.","alternatives_considered":["In-memory only state: faster but non-recoverable. Rejected — losing saga state mid-compensation could leave the system in an inconsistent state permanently."]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Step ordering","evolution_path":"The saga definition is a data structure (ordered list of steps with forward/compensate actions). Reordering steps or inserting new ones (e.g., a FraudCheck step between Payment and Courier) requires changing only the saga definition, not the services."},{"area":"Sync-to-async migration","evolution_path":"Each orchestrator-to-service call is behind an interface. Swapping synchronous HTTP calls for async message-based command/reply requires changing only the adapter layer, not the saga logic."},{"area":"Parallel steps","evolution_path":"If future requirements allow inventory reservation and payment to happen in parallel (independent steps), the saga engine can be extended to support parallel step execution with a join before the next sequential step."},{"area":"New services","evolution_path":"Adding a 5th step (e.g., NotificationService) means adding one entry to the saga step list and implementing the forward + compensate operations in the new service. Zero changes to existing services."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Compensation ordering","reason":"Compensating transactions MUST execute in strict reverse order of completed forward steps. This is a correctness invariant, not a design choice. Violating it (e.g., cancelling the order before releasing inventory) could leave dangling reservations or orphaned payments."},{"area":"Idempotency requirement","reason":"Every forward and compensating operation MUST be idempotent. This is non-negotiable — the orchestrator will retry on transient failures. Services that cannot guarantee idempotency will corrupt state on retries."},{"area":"Saga state durability","reason":"Saga state MUST be persisted to durable storage before executing each step. In-memory-only state is not acceptable — an orchestrator crash mid-saga would leave the system in an unrecoverable inconsistent state."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Compensating action itself fails (e.g., PaymentService is down when we try to refund)","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Retry compensating actions with exponential backoff up to a configurable limit (e.g., 10 retries over 30 minutes). If all retries exhausted, mark saga as COMPENSATION_FAILED and alert ops for manual intervention. Never silently give up on compensation."},{"risk":"Orchestrator crashes mid-saga","severity":"high","mitigation":"Saga state is persisted after every step transition. On restart, the orchestrator loads all ACTIVE and COMPENSATING sagas and resumes from their last persisted state. Idempotent operations ensure replaying the current step is safe."},{"risk":"Semantic inconsistency window — a customer sees a charge before courier assignment completes or fails","severity":"medium","mitigation":"This is inherent to sagas (no distributed ACID). Mitigate by keeping the window short (fast step execution), and by showing the order as 'Processing' until the saga reaches COMPLETED. If compensation occurs, proactively notify the customer that the charge will be refunded."},{"risk":"Inventory reservation leak — saga hangs and reservation is never released","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Reservation TTL as defense-in-depth. A background job in InventoryService sweeps expired reservations. Additionally, a saga timeout in the orchestrator triggers compensation if the saga has been active too long."},{"risk":"Duplicate courier assignment due to retry","severity":"medium","mitigation":"CourierService.assignCourier must be idempotent on (orderId). If called twice for the same order, return the existing assignment rather than creating a new one."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement the saga orchestrator as a lightweight state machine. Define the saga as a configuration object: an ordered array of steps, each with { name, forwardAction, compensateAction, timeout }. The orchestrator loop: load current state → execute next forward step → persist result → advance. On failure: transition to COMPENSATING → execute compensating actions in reverse → persist each → finalize as COMPENSATED. Key implementation detail: the orchestrator should be stateless (all state in the store), allowing horizontal scaling and crash recovery.","files_to_create_or_modify":["saga/SagaDefinition.ts — defines the step sequence, forward/compensate action references","saga/SagaOrchestrator.ts — state machine engine that drives execution and compensation","saga/SagaStateStore.ts — persistence interface + implementation (Postgres recommended)","saga/SagaInstance.ts — domain object representing a running saga instance","saga/steps/CreateOrderStep.ts — forward: createOrder, compensate: cancelOrder","saga/steps/ReserveInventoryStep.ts — forward: reserveItems, compensate: releaseReservation","saga/steps/ChargePaymentStep.ts — forward: chargeCustomer, compensate: refundPayment","saga/steps/AssignCourierStep.ts — forward: assignCourier, compensate: unassignCourier","saga/SagaRecoveryJob.ts — background job that resumes stuck sagas on orchestrator restart"],"patterns_to_follow":"Each step implements a SagaStep interface: { execute(context): Promise<StepResult>, compensate(context): Promise<void> }. Context carries accumulated results from prior steps (orderId, reservationId, paymentId). All operations use idempotency keys derived from sagaId + stepName. Compensating actions are fire-and-retry — they must eventually succeed or escalate to manual intervention.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT use choreography for the core compensation flow — it becomes untraceable with 4+ services","Do NOT skip persisting saga state between steps — crash recovery depends on it","Do NOT assume compensating actions will succeed on first try — always implement retry with backoff","Do NOT mix saga coordination logic with business logic — the orchestrator should be a generic engine","Do NOT forget idempotency keys on PaymentService calls — double-charging a customer is the highest-severity business failure","Do NOT use database transactions spanning multiple services — that defeats the entire purpose of the saga pattern","Do NOT allow partial compensation — if compensation of step N fails, do NOT proceed to compensate step N-1. Retry step N's compensation first. Out-of-order compensation creates inconsistent state."]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":92,"high_confidence_areas":["Orchestration over choreography for this step count and failure complexity","Compensating transaction design and ordering","Idempotency requirements across all operations","State machine representation of the saga lifecycle"],"low_confidence_areas":["Whether sync or async communication fits the actual latency/availability requirements — depends on infrastructure context not provided","Exact TTL values for inventory reservations — domain-specific tuning needed","Whether the payment model should use authorize-then-capture (2-step) vs direct capture — depends on payment gateway capabilities"],"assumptions":["Each service is independently deployable with its own data store","Network between services is unreliable (standard distributed systems assumption)","The orchestrator has access to a durable persistence layer (e.g., Postgres, DynamoDB)","Payment capture is a single-step operation (not authorize + capture) — if 2-step, the saga design simplifies because authorization is easier to void than a capture is to refund","Order volumes are moderate enough that synchronous orchestration does not become a bottleneck — at very high scale, async command/reply would be preferred"]}}
